Jesus—Lord of the Resurrection
Elder Don B. Colton
Director, Missionary Home, Salt Lake City, and Member, Sunday School General Board

"THE first day of the week cometh Mary Magdalene early, when it was yet dark, unto the sepulchre, and seeth the stone taken away from the sepulchre" (John 20:1).

Jesus had risen. It has always seemed so very appropriate that the Savior would choose to rise from the tomb just before dawn of the most wonderful Sunday in history. We can easily believe that the darkness of that night was dispelled with a morning of unusual brightness, giving color to the flowers and filling all the land with beauty and brilliance without glare. How hallowed the morning of that day! Of course, it was necessary to frighten away the Roman soldiers and that an angel should roll away the stone at the door of the tomb.

Comparatively few, however, knew of one of the most important events in all the history of the world.

Very early in the morning—without show or ostentation—the power of the Lord was made manifest. Oh, the majesty of simplicity! The stone sealing the sepulchre was removed. The disciples who came first found the tomb empty. The linen clothes which had covered the precious body of the Lord and the napkin "that was about his head" were lying neat
notes: Clear break mid-sentence where 'neat' in Misc connects with 'ly folded' in Art 1. The article title, author, and beginning are clearly present in the Misc file.
